hallucinations.description: The paper cites 'Dayan et al., 2026' with ArXiv ID 'abs/2602.01083'. This paper supposedly has a publication year of 2026, which is in the future. The ArXiv ID format '2602.01083' would correspond to February 2026, which has not occurred yet. This citation appears to be fabricated or hallucinated by the AI system.
hallucinations.evidence: A. Dayan, Yam Eitan, and Haggai Maron. On the expressive power of permutation-equivariant weight-space networks. ArXiv, abs/2602.01083, 2026.

hallucinations.description: The paper claims the sign flipping augmentation is applied to 'neurons with homogeneous activations' where 'we randomly flip signs of outgoing and incoming weights simultaneously.' However, examining the code, sign flipping is applied to ALL neurons in the first hidden layer with a random 50% probability mask, regardless of whether the activations are homogeneous. ReLU is not a homogeneous activation in the strict mathematical sense (it's only positively homogeneous), and the sign flip operation as implemented does NOT preserve functional equivalence for ReLU networks because flipping the sign of a neuron's output weights and input weights changes the function when the bias is also flipped (ReLU(ax+b) ≠ ReLU(-ax-b) in general since ReLU is not an odd function). This is actually confirmed by the paper's own functional similarity results showing a low-similarity tail.
hallucinations.evidence: Sign flipping: For neurons with homogeneous activations, we randomly flip signs of outgoing and incoming weights simultaneously.

hallucinations.description: The paper reports specific numerical results that are inconsistent with the actual experimental data. The paper states 'validation MAE (from 32.7% to 17.9% with 16 augmentations per model)' in the abstract. Looking at the actual experimental results from the code/data, the baseline MAE is 32.7071% and with 16 augmentations it's 17.8562% (from research_summary.json). However, the paper also reports in the results section 'With 16 augmentations per model, we achieve 17.9% MAE compared to 32.7% without augmentation (45% relative improvement).' The relative improvement calculation: (32.7 - 17.9)/32.7 = 45.3%, which approximately matches. But in a different run (from the epoch budget ablation), with 16 augmentations the MAE was 17.4557%, not 17.9%. The paper cherry-picks or mixes results from different experimental runs without clarification.
hallucinations.evidence: augmentation consistently improves validation MAE (from 32.7% to 17.9% with 16 augmentations per model)

hallucinations.description: The paper describes the weight matrix dimensions incorrectly in the permutation formula. It states 'W^(ℓ) ∈ R^{n_{ℓ+1} × n_ℓ}' but then the notation appears garbled with repeated subscripts ('R^{n_ℓℓ+1 × n_ℓn_ℓℓ}'). More importantly, the paper claims this is the 'first systematic study of symmetry-aware augmentation for weight-space learning', but the idea.md mentions several related works (Beiser et al., 2025; Meynent et al., 2025) that are not cited in the paper itself, and the paper only cites 5 references total, making the 'first systematic study' claim unverifiable.
hallucinations.evidence: W^(ℓ) ∈ R^{n_ℓℓ+1 × n_ℓn_ℓℓ} and bias b^(ℓ) ... Our work provides the first systematic study of symmetry-aware augmentation for weight-space learning

hallucinations.description: The paper reports specific ablation results (e.g., 'Very aggressive (scale factor 0.70–1.30): 22.79% MAE') in the appendix, but the actual experimental data from the augmentation magnitude ablation shows 'VERY_AGGRESSIVE' final_value as 22.7869. While this rounds to 22.79%, the paper also reports 'Moderate augmentation magnitude achieves best performance (21.9% MAE)' in the main text, which matches the experimental data (21.9493). However, the paper states 'very aggressive augmentation degrades to 27.2% MAE' in the main text (Section 5), but the actual data shows VERY_MILD at 27.1657 and VERY_AGGRESSIVE at 22.7869. The paper appears to confuse 'very mild' with 'very aggressive' augmentation results.
hallucinations.evidence: Moderate augmentation magnitude achieves best performance (21.9% MAE), while very aggressive augmentation degrades to 27.2% MAE

overall_assessment: The paper contains several hallucinations of varying severity. The most concerning is a nonexistent citation (Dayan et al., 2026) with a future date and fabricated ArXiv ID. There are also methodological inaccuracies regarding the sign flipping operation's theoretical justification, and some experimental results appear to be misreported or confused between different ablation conditions (particularly confusing 'very mild' and 'very aggressive' augmentation results). The paper's core experimental trends (augmentation improves MAE) are supported by the code, but specific numerical claims and theoretical descriptions contain errors.
